title: Spider and Other Insect Bites & Stings
synonyms: Spider and Other Insect Bites & Stings

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Spider bites are common and rarely result in a serious reaction unless the patient has an underlying allergy to the venom.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- In susceptible individual’s allergy/anaphylaxis may occur. Watch for respiratory and gastrointestinal symptoms and treat for anaphylaxis if identified
- Intense localised pain, redness and swelling at the site. Pain may become intense and spread
- Nausea, vomiting and abdominal pain
- Tender glands in the groin or armpit of envenomed limb

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Redback spider bites are usually painful but rarely life-threatening; severe pain, systemic symptoms, or significant envenomation require medical assessment
- Funnel-web spider envenomation is a medical emergency and should be managed with pressure immobilisation and urgent transport
- Pressure immobilisation is not recommended for redback spider bites because it may worsen pain and has not been shown to improve outcome
- Bee, wasp, and ant stings can cause local reactions or anaphylaxis and require close observation
- Avoid routine antibiotic or steroid treatment from this guideline unless another clear indication exists

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Keep patient at rest and observe for signs of anaphylaxis
- Conduct Vital Sign Survey and be alert for deterioration
- For bee stings, remove the sting as quickly as possible by whatever method is readily available
- Use a cold compress to reduce pain and swelling
- Consider simple analgesia if indicated
- For suspected funnel-web spider bite, apply pressure immobilisation and arrange urgent transport
- For redback spider bite, use ice or cold compress and symptomatic pain relief; do not use pressure immobilisation

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ECG if clinically indicated
- Consider IV access if severe pain, anaphylaxis, or systemic toxicity is present
- Observe closely for signs of anaphylaxis and treat accordingly if present
- Consider stronger analgesia for severe pain, including redback spider bites, in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Escalate if there is spreading pain, systemic toxicity, or concern for funnel-web envenomation
- Consider tetanus review where appropriate

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Children with insect-sting anaphylaxis may present with vomiting or diarrhoea early
- Pressure immobilisation is reserved for suspected funnel-web spider bite and is not used for redback spider bites
- The Sydney funnel-web is not known to be established in WA, but suspected funnel-web envenomation should still be managed as an emergency
